pages.md:
Automated Contrast Enhancement of Mammograms for Early Cancer Detection

# Abstract

For achieving earlier detection of breast cancer (resulting in higher clinical utility), a complete automated analysis of mammographic images is necessary. In this work, we propose a general DIP pipeline for breast cancer classification based on the CBIS-DDSM database. It is comprised of two stages: preprocessing (using bilateral filtering for noise reduction, CLAHE on both pass-directions, and an adaptive thresholding based method to segment the lesion) and a Random Forest classifier. The classification is achieved with 15 hand-crafted features separated in shape, intensity and textures (GLCM). Good classification performance is achieved when the data (train and test images are equally balanced and there are 1000 images per class) is analyzed by 5-fold stratified cross-validation. We want to make the system explainable and thus, we provide the entire test-set, the ROC curve, and the permutation importance in order to provide good confidence of the classification results on an image.

# Index Terms

CBIS-DDSM, Bilateral Filtering, CLAHE, Adaptive Thresholding, Random Forest Classifier, GLCM, ROC Curve.

# I. INTRODUCTION

Breast cancer is the most frequent type of cancer in women worldwide, accounting for almost 25% of all cancers in women and a smaller proportion of cancers overall. Despite various treatment methods, high mortality rates correlate highly with late-stage detection. Population-based screening using systematic mammographic views has significantly lowered the mortality rates by 15%-30% with the assistance of an image quality based evaluation system. The computational challenge presented by digital mammograms is due to the poor contrast and similarities between pathological characteristics (masses, calcifications, Architectural Distortions) and complex glandular tissue. This has produced two main challenges for automatic interpretation: (1) the inherently low contrast between the lesions and the surrounding tissue and (2) high variability in morphology and texture between different individuals. Thus, effective preprocessing and accurate segmentation are crucial steps prior to any feature extraction and detection algorithms. While current research is predominantly using deep learning, traditional handcrafted feature-based approaches have remained an important and computationally feasible solution; their ensemble application with classifiers like Random Forest ensures clinical explainability along with competitive detection performance. In this paper we present:

- A Multi-stage DIP Pipeline: incorporating bilateral filtering, double pass CLAHE, adaptive Gaussian thresholding, and morphological closing/opening stages for lesion isolation.

# II. RELATED WORK

# A. Standardizing with the CBIS-DDSM Standard

All current CAD systems utilize the CBIS-DDSM dataset, which is an enhanced version of the original DDSM database. It contains everything from original DICOM files to professionally drawn ROI boxes in a comprehensive environment to train classifiers against and measure against as the world’s standard of care.

# B. Sophisticated Contrast Strategies

Low contrast imaging is a serious problem in mammography. Though CLAHE is the most common image enhancement utilized, we take the ideas of Pizer and Reza further, utilizing a double-pass technique of enhancement that essentially multiplies the feature visibility, so that any masses’ irregular margins are distinct enough for the segmentation stage to detect.

# C. Segmentation

We do not rely on global thresholds, which perform poorly on dense images, but rather use an adaptive Gaussian thresholding which allows the threshold values to be determined on a per-image basis. We then run morphological post-processing on the resultant masks to “clean up” the binary masks, removing any gaps in masses and deleting the small noise clusters that result from image analysis.

# D. Interpretable Feature Engineering

Though deep learning is effective, it is not as transparent as handcrafted features (i.e. Shape eccentricity, GLCM features). We used handcrafted features in the Random Forest algorithm which allows for robust decision making with features whose individual importance can be mathematically proven and clinically interpreted.

pages.md:
# III. DATASET

CBIS-DDSM (Curated Breast Imaging Subset of DDSM), which contains a total number of screening mammography examination cases partitioned in benign and malignant. In our pipeline, the dataset’s location will automatically be found from several root paths. MAX = 1, 000 is used which contains at most 1000 images for each class. For a fallback scenario in a disconnected system, an automatic synthetic dataset fallback mechanism will work. 5,000 samples of a balanced set (2500 for each class) will be generated using mathematically prescribed morphological priors mimicking some aspects of clinical ROI.

# B. Segmentation

The segment_tumor() module isolates the pathological region using:

| TABLE I                                                                                                    |           |         |              |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------- | ------- | ------------ |
| DATASET CONFIGURATION. THE SYSTEM AUTO-DETECTS AVAILABILITY AND SWITCHES BETWEEN REAL AND SYNTHETIC MODES. |           |         |              |
| Split / Mode                                                                                               | Class     | Samples | Notes        |
| Real (CBIS-DDSM)                                                                                           | Benign    | ≤ 1,000 | JPEG / DICOM |
| Real (CBIS-DDSM)                                                                                           | Malignant | ≤ 1,000 | JPEG / DICOM |

# IV. METHODOLOGY

The diagnostic pipeline consists of six successive stages: image loading, preprocessing, lesion segmentation, morphological post-processing, feature extraction, and Random Forest classification.

# A. Preprocessing Pipeline

The preprocess() function implements a four-stage enhancement sequence:

1. Bilateral Denoising (d = 9, σColor = 75, σSpace = 75): Smooths homogeneous regions while preserving critical edge gradients.
2. Median Filtering (kernel size=3): Specifically targets residual salt-and-pepper noise.
3. Double-Pass CLAHE (clipLimit = 2.0, tileGridSize = 8 × 8): Applied sequentially; the first pass stabilizes global contrast, while the second enhances localized intensity variations.
4. Normalization: Rescales pixel intensities to a [0, 1] float32 range.
5. Otsu Thresholding: Otsu’s method finds an optimal global threshold value by maximizing the between-class variance of foreground and background pixels. It works on the assumption that intensity distribution is bimodal and calculates a threshold value that optimally separates the two classes. It is used for initial lesion segmentation of mammographic images.
6. Adaptive Thresholding: Adaptive thresholding assigns a local threshold for each pixel on the basis of distribution of pixel intensity in its neighborhood. Gaussian weighted averaging is implemented to adapt illumination variance. This technique is suitable for medical images where illumination is non-uniform throughout the image.

# C. Feature Extraction

The extract_feature() function computes a 15-dimensional feature vector from the preprocessed ROI and its corresponding binary mask. These features characterize the lesion across three primary domains: shape morphology, density statistics, and GLCM-based texture descriptors. Detailed definitions and their clinical significance are provided in Table II.

pages.md:
# TABLE II

# COMPLETE 15-FEATURE DESCRIPTOR SET WITH DEFINITIONS AND CLINICAL RELEVANCE

| #  | Feature          | Definition                           | Clinical Relevance             |
| -- | ---------------- | ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------ |
| 1  | Area             | Total pixel count inside lesion mask | Malignant lesions often larger |
| 2  | Perimeter        | Arc length of lesion contour         | Boundary length descriptor     |
| 3  | Circularity      | 4π × Area / Perimeter2               | Regular vs. irregular shape    |
| 4  | Compactness      | Perimeter2 / Area                    | Higher for irregular lesions   |
| 5  | Solidity         | Area / Convex Hull Area              | Margin convexity               |
| 6  | Roughness        | Perimeter2 / Area                    | Boundary texture proxy         |
| 7  | Aspect Ratio     | Width / Height of bounding box       |                                |
| 8  | Extent           | Area / Bounding box area             |                                |
| 9  | Mean Intensity   | Average ROI pixel intensity          | Elongation of mass             |
| 10 | Intensity Std    | Std. dev. of intensity               |                                |
| 11 | Skewness         | 3rd moment of distribution           |                                |
| 12 | GLCM Contrast    | Contrast (spatial variation)         |                                |
| 13 | GLCM Energy      | Energy (uniformity)                  |                                |
| 14 | GLCM Homogeneity | Homogeneity (smoothness)             |                                |
| 15 | GLCM Correlation | Correlation                          |                                |

D. Random Forest Classifier

Classification is performed via an optimized learning pipeline using a RandomForestClassifier (n estimators = 200, weight='balanced'). The data is partitioned into an 80/20 train-test split, with the training set subject to stratified 5-fold cross-validation. To maintain clinical interpretability, Permutation Importance (n repeats = 10) is calculated on the test set to rank feature contribution.

Following extraction, the discriminative power of these features was validated through distribution analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 3 and Fig. 4, shape-based descriptors such as solidity and circularity provide the highest degree of class separation.

# A. Random Forest Results Dashboard

Figure 5 presents the complete evaluation dashboard including the confusion matrix, ROC curve with AUC, validation accuracy distribution, and permutation-based feature importance ranking.

MALIGNANT Coefficient 98.2%

Fig. 3. Feature distribution histograms for all 15 features comparing Benign (green) vs. Malignant (red) classes. Shape features show the strongest separation.

The visual evidence provided by the histograms in Fig. 3 confirms that several handcrafted features possess high discriminative power. Additionally, the correlation analysis in Fig. 4 justifies the inclusion of both intensity and texture descriptors.

pages.md:
# B. Classifier Performance Matrics

# E. Comparison with Published Methods

| CLASSIFICATION PERFORMANCE AND INFERENCE METRICS |        |                             |
| :----------------------------------------------: | ------ | --------------------------- |
|                      Metric                      | Value  | Note                        |
|                5-Fold CV Accuracy                | 91.25% | Mean ± std over 5 folds     |
|                 Test Set Accuracy                | 91.25% | Held-out 20% split          |
|                 Precision (macro)                | 90.70% | TTP / (TP + FP)             |
|                  Recall (macro)                  | 91.92% | TTP / (TP + FN)             |
|                 F1 Score (macro)                 | 91.31% | Harmonic mean P & R         |
|                      AUC-ROC                     | 90.58% | Area under ROC curve        |
|              Single-image Confidence             | 91.33% | Predicted probability score |

# C. Single Image Interference

The result of a complete single-image inference is shown in Figure 6. The input image before preprocessing is also visualized along with the segmentation mask, feature values, and the predicted panel where the prediction is MALIGNANT with confidence score of 91.33%.

Fig. 6. Single-image inference result — PREDICTION: MALIGNANT, CONFIDENCE: 91.33%. The panel displays the input image, segmentation mask, and the 15 extracted DIP feature values.

# APPLICATIONS AND LIMITATIONS

# A. Clinical Applications

The proposed pipeline is suitable for deployment as a second-reader tool in screening mammography programs, particularly in resource-constrained settings (e.g., rural telemedicine, mobile units) where GPU-based deep learning inference is impractical. The 15-feature vector maps directly to established BI-RADS descriptors (shape, margin character, texture), enabling radiologists to audit and contextualize each automated decision. Permutation importance rankings provide data-driven confirmation of which image features drive classification, thereby increasing interpretability and supporting regulatory compliance.

# B. Limitations

- Results reflect the synthetic dataset (5,000 samples) when CBIS-DDSM is unavailable; real-data metrics may differ due to domain shift.
- The 15-feature set omits calcification-specific descriptors (e.g., granulometry, sparkle patterns), limiting applicability to calcification-type lesions.
- Adaptive thresholding parameters (blockSize = 35, C = 5) are tuned for 224×224 JPEG ROIs; other resolutions may require re-tuning.
- No data augmentation is applied during training; the model may be sensitive to class imbalance in real-world deployment.

# CONCLUSION

A complete automated digital image processing pipeline was proposed and presented that allows automated enhancement and breast cancer classification of mammography images on the CBIS-DDSM dataset. By combining the chain of multi-step pre-processing (bilateral filtering for noise reduction, double pass CLAHE, and morphological opening/closing) with a set of 15 handcrafted feature descriptions, the pipeline maintains a high degree of diagnostic transparency. The Random Forest classifier model yields a highly reliable confidence score of 91.33 percent during single-image inference. As opposed to the conventional "black-box" approach to these.

# D. Extracted Feature Values for Test Sample

| EXTRACTED 15-FEATURE VECTOR FOR THE MALIGNANT TEST SAMPLE (CONFIDENCE: 91.33%) |           |                             |
| :----------------------------------------------------------------------------: | --------- | --------------------------- |
|                                     Feature                                    | Value     | Interpretation              |
|                                      Area                                      | 25,510.50 | Large lesion region         |
|                                    Perimeter                                   | 989.55    | Extensive boundary          |
|                                   Circularity                                  | 0.3274    | Low — irregular shape       |
|                                   Compactness                                  | 3.0545    | High — spiculated margin    |
|                                    Solidity                                    | 0.7200    | Non-convex boundary         |
|                               Boundary Roughness                               | 3.0545    | High roughness              |
|                                  Aspect Ratio                                  | 1.3494    | Slightly elongated          |
|                                     Extent                                     | 0.6986    | Moderate fill ratio         |
|                                 Mean Intensity                                 | 128.09    | Mid-range brightness        |
|                                  Std Intensity                                 | 62.58     | High internal heterogeneity |
|                                    Skewness                                    | 0.1261    | Slight positive skew        |
|                                  GLCM Contrast                                 | 4.0006    | High local variation        |
|                                   GLCM Energy                                  | 0.3180    | Moderate uniformity         |
|                                GLCM Homogeneity                                | 0.8311    | Moderate smoothness         |
|                                GLCM Correlation                                | 0.9787    | High spatial dependency     |

pages.text:
problems, the workflow is readily inspectable (or auditable) via
the various intermediary outputs presented during processing,
thereby allowing for integration into clinical workflow for
decision support purposes. Future work will concentrate on
adding calcification specific feature descriptors, developing a
hybrid of classical and deep-learn based networks, and  p-
plying Bayesian hyper-parameter optimization to the existing
models. In addition, a DICOM-native REST API system will
be designed to allow the models to be called, and clinical
evaluation is sought.

                           REFERENCES

 [1]  S. M. Lee et al., “A Curated Mammography Data Set for Use in
      Computer-Aided Detection and Diagnosis Research,” GigaScience, vol.
      6, no. 11, 2017.
 [2]  M. Heath et al., “The Digital Database for Screening Mammography,”
      Proc. 5th Int. Workshop Digital Mammography, 2000, pp. 212–218.
 [3]  S. Pizer et al., “Adaptive histogram equalization and its variations,”
      Comput. Vision Graph. Image Process., vol. 39, no. 3, pp. 355–368,
      1987.
 [4]  A. M. Reza, “Realization of the Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram
      Equalization (CLAHE) for real-time image enhancement,” J. VLSI
      Signal Process., vol. 38, no. 1, pp. 35–44, 2004.
 [5]  N. Otsu, “A Threshold Selection Method from Gray-Level Histograms,”
      IEEE Trans. Syst. Man Cybern., vol. 9, no. 1, pp. 62–66, 1979.
 [6]  R. M. Haralick, K. Shanmugam, and I. Dinstein, “Textural features for
      image classification,” IEEE Trans. Syst. Man Cybern., vol. SMC-3, no.
      6, pp. 610–621, 1973.
 [7]  L. Breiman, “Random Forests,” Machine Learning, vol. 45, no. 1, pp.
      5–32, 2001.
 [8]  A. Altmann et al., “Permutation importance: a corrected feature  mp  -
      tance measure,” Bioinformatics, vol. 26, no. 10, pp. 1340–1347, 2010.
 [9]  C. Tomasi and R. Manduchi, “Bilateral filtering for gray and color
      images,” Proc. IEEE ICCV, 1998, pp. 839–846.
[10]  F. Pedregosa et al., “Scikit-learn: Machine learning in Python,” J. Mach.
      Learn. Res., vol. 12, pp. 2825–2830, 2011.
[11]  R. C. Gonzalez and R. E. Woods, Digital Image Processing, 4th ed.
      Pearson, 2018.
[12]  K. He, X. Zhang, S. Ren, and J. Sun, “Deep residual learning for image
      recognition,” Proc. IEEE CVPR, 2016, pp. 770–778.
pages.md:
problems, the workflow is readily inspectable (or auditable) via the various intermediary outputs presented during processing, thereby allowing for integration into clinical workflow for decision support purposes. Future work will concentrate on adding calcification specific feature descriptors, developing a hybrid of classical and deep-learn based networks, and applying Bayesian hyper-parameter optimization to the existing models. In addition, a DICOM-native REST API system will be designed to allow the models to be called, and clinical evaluation is sought.

# REFERENCES

1. S. M. Lee et al., “A Curated Mammography Data Set for Use in Computer-Aided Detection and Diagnosis Research,” GigaScience, vol. 6, no. 11, 2017.
2. M. Heath et al., “The Digital Database for Screening Mammography,” Proc. 5th Int. Workshop Digital Mammography, 2000, pp. 212–218.
3. S. Pizer et al., “Adaptive histogram equalization and its variations,” Comput. Vision Graph. Image Process., vol. 39, no. 3, pp. 355–368, 1987.
4. A. M. Reza, “Realization of the Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for real-time image enhancement,” J. VLSI Signal Process., vol. 38, no. 1, pp. 35–44, 2004.
5. N. Otsu, “A Threshold Selection Method from Gray-Level Histograms,” IEEE Trans. Syst. Man Cybern., vol. 9, no. 1, pp. 62–66, 1979.
6. R. M. Haralick, K. Shanmugam, and I. Dinstein, “Textural features for image classification,” IEEE Trans. Syst. Man Cybern., vol. SMC-3, no. 6, pp. 610–621, 1973.
7. L. Breiman, “Random Forests,” Machine Learning, vol. 45, no. 1, pp. 5–32, 2001.
8. A. Altmann et al., “Permutation importance: a corrected feature importance measure,” Bioinformatics, vol. 26, no. 10, pp. 1340–1347, 2010.
9. C. Tomasi and R. Manduchi, “Bilateral filtering for gray and color images,” Proc. IEEE ICCV, 1998, pp. 839–846.
10. F. Pedregosa et al., “Scikit-learn: Machine learning in Python,” J. Mach. Learn. Res., vol. 12, pp. 2825–2830, 2011.
11. R. C. Gonzalez and R. E. Woods, Digital Image Processing, 4th ed. Pearson, 2018.
12. K. He, X. Zhang, S. Ren, and J. Sun, “Deep residual learning for image recognition,” Proc. IEEE CVPR, 2016, pp. 770–778.

items.md: problems, the workflow is readily inspectable (or auditable) via the various intermediary outputs presented during processing, thereby allowing for integration into clinical workflow for decision support purposes. Future work will concentrate on adding calcification specific feature descriptors, developing a hybrid of classical and deep-learn based networks, and applying Bayesian hyper-parameter optimization to the existing models. In addition, a DICOM-native REST API system will be designed to allow the models to be called, and clinical evaluation is sought.
